All right, what's up everybody? I'm joined here by my good friend with the beautiful eyes right now staring into the camera. I don't know if you see that, but I'm here with Scott Oldford and there are so many ways I can introduce this man to you. You know, where I really, really resonate with him is on the concept of coaching the conversion. If you're in 10X launches, you've seen that play out when it comes to email copy. Scott takes that concept and he does that across the whole funnel and everything from lead generation to how to offer experiences to your customer all the way through that buying journey, all the way into acquisition, into raving fans. So that's what really amazes me about his process, but what I love most about Scott is that when I discovered him and his material about a year ago, it gave me permission to seriously and I kid you not unsubscribe from every other marketing list out there. He is a genius at this craft. He's one of the most adventurous marketers I know, really willing to push that envelope of what's possible and what we can do to really coach that conversion. So with all that said, Scott, awesome having you here, man. Awesome to be here, man. You know, I'm going to try to live up to that introduction, but we'll see what happens in the next half an hour or so. Yeah. It's quite the billing. I didn't know the intro would be so long. We should have had you behind the red curtains and then have you pop up to this massive standing ovation. Exactly. Sweet. So I'm going to start off with like an underhand pitch here and just give you like the easiest question of the day. What excites you most about being a marketer in 2017? Well, I think number one, I'm not really a marketer, which is probably the exciting part about marketing is when you're a marketer, you get to break a lot of shit. And I'm really good at like breaking things. I'm really good at just like, you know, being like, how can we do this? I look at marketing in 2017 and the way that we're marketing is going. It's so easy to be personalized, right? And so, you know, I have a book that's going to be coming out sometime in the future. I don't know when. And, you know, it's based on the concept of being relevant. And marketing is really being relevant to the right person at the right time. And if we do that, then a sale becomes an opportunity, right? And as soon as we have an opportunity, we want to jump on an opportunity. How many people actually want a sales pitch? Nobody. How many people want an opportunity? Everybody. So if we can craft that opportunity and we can craft that person to wanting the opportunity, we have a very, very easy way to be able to market our business, to be able to engage and to understand where somebody is in the buying mindset and be able to coach them towards really the point of saying, yes, I want that. Which when it comes from a launch perspective, you know, one of the biggest problems I've always seen with launches in the past, and why I think that, you know, the model has been needed to have some reinvention is the fact that, you know, anytime we push somebody, we're really going to do it ourselves at this justice, right? Because if we're pushing a bunch of people with the same thing over and over and over again, and everybody gets the same experience, as I personalize, and instead of pulling or as, you know, our mayor friend Jesse Alder talks about illuminating their pain and that being able to bring them through the pain to the vision, to the gap, to the commitment, if we don't do that, then we lose a lot of people. So, the most majority of the leads we generate, we're going to lose if we push them. But if we pull them through an experience, and we adapt our messaging based on where they're to from their mindset, we have an incredible advantage where we can actually be super relevant to them. And whenever someone or something is relevant, it's kind of like that light bulb switch goes off in our head where it's like, you know what, I want to follow this person, I want to invest in this person, I want to buy this person's product, or I want to, you know, hire them as a coach or whatever it is, okay? That's right. And so, yeah, so that's really, like, that's why I, you know, I don't resonate as a marketer. I don't even probably resonate as an entrepreneur to be completely honest. I just, I love being able to kind of see those kind of switches go off in people's minds, and be able to create a systematic procedure behind being able to get somebody's behavior the way that allows them to work with us and allows us as entrepreneurs to, you know, generate customers that are our perfect customers, because they're ready to be our customers, right? They're not ready for the solution, they're ready to be our customers. So yeah, so that's definitely what excites me, you know, I don't care about all the tools and all the, you know, the platforms and all that type of stuff. I think it's a lot of noise. Yeah, I mean, I just, I'm having, I'm having a lot of fun just, you know, kind of just having fun, you know? Yeah, that's awesome. What I'm hearing here and what I've certainly experienced participating in your marketing funnels is that relevancy is a gift. Relevancy is giving value, you know, we get so wrapped up in this idea that the only way to give value to a customer or a prospect is through free gifts, a lead magnet, a webinar, whatever, but literally illuminating, you know, that process for them, illuminating where those pain points are and diagnosing them properly is one of the best gifts you can give them, that empathy and that relevancy. So is that something you've experienced as you've implemented these funnels, you know, hundreds or thousands of times by now? Yeah, I mean, you know, I think, I think at the end of the day, you know, our potential customers are here and our job is to bring them here or there, you know, wherever it particularly is. And I mean, you know, at some point, all entrepreneurs serve people, right? And you know, if I talk to a lot of entrepreneurs and, you know, majority of entrepreneurs that are under the quarter a million are struggling, right? You know, between that zero to quarter a million is the most difficult part of the entrepreneurial journey. And one of the biggest things that I see is that the reason why those entrepreneurs stay under a quarter million is because they're not leveraging their ability to serve and not leveraging the amount of impact they could have out there. So you know, you see a lot of people that don't have marketing funnels that don't have, you know, they have a lead magnet that, you know, they just have a webinar or they just have a lead magnet. They're really kind of only, you know, talking to the 2% of the market that everybody else around is talking to. And in reality, you know, if you talk to 98%, you get a heck of a lot more money and you have a heck of a lot more impact and you have an audience and all these different types of things. You know, four years ago, I was 700K in debt. And one of the things that I realized when I was $700,000 in debt was the fact that, you know, one of the things that nobody can take from you are relationships slash your audience, okay? And so I see a lot of business owners trying to get sales instead of trying to create a community. And if you create a community, you automatically get sales because you solve the problem in the community and there's a percentage in the debt community that typically is larger than 2% that one will work with you and will buy from you and will want to have to be served at the higher level, which is why I'm you just community building, which is why I'm you John building an audience because I guarantee you once you build an audience, it's really hard to fail. It's really hard to go bankrupt. You have an audience of people that know I can trust you. And so and you're able to give them that relevant experience based on where they're to, right? So that's why I mean, you do, you do, you believe are of the community side. And I'm no buy life, no far a people person. Okay. I'm like the last person I would have ever expected to be running communities and like, you know, audiences, all this different type of stuff. But to me is the first sure way to make sure that you are future proofing yourself and you're making a risk inside your business. And you know, I've done incredibly well to get this right. Right. Right. So here's a question. So people who are kind of struggling with audience building right now, people who are probably also creating awesome content and sharing those insights and not afraid to do that, but still not getting that kind of traction. What does it take right now to stand out like you and I know you really have this full expression you allow your ethos to shine through in your content and it really, really either resonates or doesn't resonate. But is that something that you feel needs to be an essential piece of audience building right now to really think, I think there's a couple of things that comes to, you know, number one, nobody falls anybody unless they have and talk about their big why. Okay. People are addicted to other people's why is because what we have to realize that majority of people walk around in being in a level of, you know, mediocracy. Okay. So we are addicted as human beings to follow people that bigger why is that resonate with our world view. Okay. So this is why I believe it's very important for you as a business owner and an entrepreneur to have a world view, not saying that you're not like to me, my beliefs in my world view changes as new data is I'm the biggest hypocrite ever, you know what I mean? Like you give me new data that is conclusive and I will change my world view. No problem whatsoever. Okay. So I'm not attached to my world view. However, I believe it's really important that people know exactly who you are that you are completely authentic that you are that you bleed, right? And I believe along with that it comes down to frequency. So being what I talk about is being omnipresent and, you know, further than that, making it so that your content isn't just content. There's a story behind it, which of course, you know, really comes into the realm of writing and the realm of copywriting and the realm of emotional connection. I think you combine those things together. It's really quick. It's very, it happens really quickly in the audience. Is it quick like three months quick? No. You know, I have an audience of maybe about 75,000 entrepreneurs at this point, but it's taking me up. It's only taking me three years. It's taking me less than three years to do that, right? And so, you know, I look at it and you have to be coherent in your messaging. You have to be authentic. You can't be just inventing stories or inventing, you know, who you are. You can't be like, you know, I would say my business blew up where I was like, dude, this is who I am. If you don't like me, I respect that. I respect the fact that you don't like me. But this is who I am and this is what I stand for. This is what I'm going for. And you know, if others resonate with you, well, listen, man, there's lots of other entrepreneurs and marketers out there that you can listen to. I'm not the only one. And I think that that's where a lot of people, a lot of people try to serve way too many people. I'm polarizing. You know what I mean? Like you either, there's very little in between. You either love me or you hate me. There's very few people that stay in between, you know, who I am largely because of the fact that I don't put up with bullshit and I call bullshit out really quickly. And I believe that business should be done a certain way. And if you don't align with that, I don't want to know you. Right. And so I almost occurred a bubble. I almost created a bubble that I'm inside of that, hey, this is how business is done for me and what makes me happy. And if you're not cool with being in that bubble, awesome. But if you want to be in the bubble, sweet, that's cool. Let's do some bullshit together. And so a lot of people are not comfortable with that largely because majority of entrepreneurs and this is a complete mindset thing, you know, we're afraid of rejection. We're very, very afraid of rejection. We're afraid of people calling us out online or calling us out in general. We want to be liked and we are significant driven people, right? Entrepreneurs are significant driven, egotistical individuals and it's really difficult to get past that, right? The majority of people that are entrepreneurs and if you really, really, really did, they got to it. The number one reason why someone becomes an entrepreneur is because at some point in their life, whether it was when they were a kid or when they were a teenager or in their job or whatever, they felt insignificant or they feel insignificant. Okay. So what do you do? You go out and you create a situation where you're the most significant person in the room. And how do you do that? Well, the easiest way in our society right now in 2017 is become an entrepreneur. I want to attract so many people to becoming an entrepreneur, which is good and not good at the same time. It can be used as a superpower and as a huge weakness as a kryptonite. And because of that, it creates a lot of issues when you go to create a community because what you see is whenever significance is in play, you're serving other people because it serves yourself. And that's a dangerous territory to be in. And this also comes as a fact that if you're building a business to serve your significance, then it means there's something else prevailed off balance in your life and you will only be able to grow your company as much as your significance increases. And most of us get very comfortable with the amount of significance we have when it's based upon our business. And I know this because I live it for the last 15 years and I only discovered it two years ago. So like I'm the biggest, you know, 100%. That was me. Still probably is me. But that is one of the biggest hurdles when it comes to building an audience in a community is being able to get past that yourself. Right. So that's a huge point there. And you know, it's a huge inner job, you know, and we've heard the quote so many times, you know, entrepreneurship is a path of self growth and self development. And that does resonate as true. But because we have this challenge really to put ourselves out there to express ourselves fully and to actually be authentic beyond a buzzword, which actually just kind of like that probably aggravates me the most is for all the attention, the word authenticity has gotten over the last five years, how low that bar still is. And, you know, acknowledging that there are no quick fixes to this, that it is a journey, that it is something that you want to make gradual, yeah, gradual upgrades as you go along. Is there something that has really kind of like helped you make that switch, you know, any kind of reminder you give yourself any kind of practice you have anytime you feel that like tightening that constriction around putting yourself out there on such a polarizing level. Crying in the shower. Crying in the shower. Yeah. I would say a lot of what I write, not anymore, because I'm kind of like in the process of becoming an open book. It hurts, right? You're letting go of things that, and you know, there's a difference between being authentic and just putting your shit out there. A lot of people just put their shit out there. You know, I don't personally believe that when it comes to at least personal stuff, you should resolve your shit before you put it out there in a story. I see a lot of times people like try to be authentic, but when reality, they're just kind of using Facebook or their online personalities for like therapy sessions. Go to your audience. Yeah, yeah, yeah, exactly. So yeah, go to your therapist for that, and that's totally fine. But I think there's a piece of embarrassment. I think there's a piece of, you know, think, what are people going to think? You know, all of these different thoughts. And so what I've always done, whenever I've, you know, number one, whenever I write, I bleed. And number two, if I ever am in a situation, and it doesn't happen a lot anymore, because I've de-attached myself from who I am when it comes from a perspective like, this is who I am. Yeah, I'm totally fine with everybody knowing who I am, what I'm about, you know, so on and so forth. It also probably comes from the fact that I make a lot of money from being who I am at this point. So it's a little bit different now. But when I was it, it came from the fact of posting it, turning off Facebook, uninstalling it from my phone, and just going into the shower, you know, maybe crying for 30 or 45 minutes. And just literally emotionally releasing that. And so, and that sounds like a little, like totally, like, away from business, but I guarantee you, like, that has what has gotten me emotionally to where I'm to. And I think it also allows you to build your emotional immune system, right? And so I think emotional immune system is really, really important for an entrepreneur, because our emotional immune system is tested. And it's tested constantly. And I guarantee you as an entrepreneur, especially if you're a personality brand, you know, if you have a brand of your personality, and your personal brand, I think it's really important to be who you truly are. Because at some point, you will, if you personally brand yourself different than you actually are as a person, what's going to happen is you're going to come to a point of reckoning where you are somebody, okay, that you are somebody more time than you are your actual self. Somebody will encompass your actual self, and then you're going to wake up one day and you're not going to be happy with your life. Because now you can't be who you want to be anymore. And I think from a fulfillment perspective, I've been there before, right? I've been there about seven years ago. I've been there before, and I guarantee you, I would never want that for anybody. I would never wish that on, I don't really have any enemies, but if I did, I would never wish it on my worst enemy. And it plays with your head in so many different ways. You start to feel like a fraud. You know, all of these different types of things. So, you know, majority of us as well, people are attracted to authenticity, and people understand authenticity. And they understand fakeness versus being authentic and being a fake authentic. And I guarantee you, people are way more attracted to me as a person when I'm just like, listen, this is how it is. And me being like, listen, you suck, or like you are amazing, or whatever, and actually being like, yeah, you know what, that's exactly how I feel. So I think that's important, and I don't think enough, like this is all about entrepreneurship stuff, all business stuff. I don't think enough people talk about any of this. They talk about tactics and strategies and all these different types of things. But this is what allows you to build an audience, to build a following, and actually build a business that you enjoy versus one that you hate. Right. And a majority of entrepreneurs end up building businesses they hate. Right? Exactly. Majority of entrepreneurs that I know that have built their multi-million dollar business, hate their businesses. Right? Hate their businesses. And it's because of the fact that they didn't build it upon their authentic self. They built it on what they thought they needed to do. Right. To become successful. Sweet. I want to just emphasize here that this is actually a marketing lesson. You know, we aren't talking about tactics here, but even being willing to go to these places, acknowledge them within yourself and get over the hurdles and expressing them, that develops this empathy muscle that allows you to also in turn tap into what your audience is experiencing. Not just on like kind of mining for data and seeing kind of like the superficial stuff, but if you could go subcutaneous in yourself and your own pain and the tension and give a narrative and voice to that, it also helps you on the flip side do that with your audience and communicate in a way that really makes them feel seen and heard. So I think that's actually a really good point because a lot of people say, Scott, you're an amazing writer, which just going to say, I think it's bullshit. I think if I'm an amazing writer, the love for amazing writers are like really low, right? But the reason I think that people resonate with what I write and what I talk about is because I have such a level of understanding of empathy because the thing about empathy is out of sight out of mind. Okay. Because at the end of the day, you're only really empathetic about the things that are in front of you. Okay. Empathy vanishes very quickly. Okay. As human beings, we're pretty obsessed with ourselves and our immediate surroundings. Okay. And so one of the reasons I think I resonate and I think that you listening to this have the ability to resonate is if you're truly honest with who you are and your authentic self, your ability to access empathy and be able to understand how to then utilize an empathy to attract and be relevant to your audience, you don't have to be the world's most amazing writer. You'll be able to allow that person to feel like, wow, that's me. They're writing to me. They're talking to me right now. And when that moment happens, that's when you can actually attract somebody and then deliver value to that person. Right? There's a really good point in there of the fact that, yeah, this is all very much the behind the stuff things of marketing that unfortunately marketers don't talk about. Right. Right. Absolutely. And yeah, if you are watching this and you haven't done the moment of highest tension exercise, highly suggest you do that. There's a reason I've said before that I don't write until I feel like, shit. That's what gives me access to feeling what my audience is feeling in that exact moment and be so desperate to solve it because I'm solving it for myself as well. I'm solving my own experience of my customer's problems. So that's another way I really encourage everybody to use empathy, you know, be as desperate to solve your audience's problem as you would be to solve your own as if it was your own. You know, FOMO, we've said that before, shouldn't just be something you ask your audience to feel. It should be something you literally feel yourself when you sense their chance to fix that problem is slipping away. And that is something I see in a lot of what Scott does. I sense your own fear of me missing out on that opportunity. Now, I feel that really comes across and is absolutely genuine when it comes to how you communicate. I get literally pissed off at the fact that people don't. I literally get like, because like at the end of the day, like it's really not going to matter if someone buys my, you know, $2,000 product when I'm on a webinar. You know what I mean? At the end of the day, I've gone past the point where that sale is going to make her break me. But I literally get pissed off because I see people and I see what their struggle is. And I know it's super like they're not this long way is the far apart, right? They're so close and they just, they create some mental picture in their head, mental movie of what life is like versus what life is really like. And I mean, it also pissed me off when people have these, you know, totally bullshit themselves because we're really great as human beings to bullshit ourselves. Thinking that situations are worse than they are or actually better than they are. And majority of entrepreneurs, here's the most amazing thing. You would think that majority of entrepreneurs would think that the situation is worse than it actually is. But majority of entrepreneurs think their situation is better. Okay. They think their business is better than it actually is, but they think their life is worse than it actually is. Okay. So it's a parallel. People think their finances, their marketing, their sales, their delivery, their customer service and their operations are better than they actually are in complete reality. And when it comes to their life, love, relationships, they're all those different types of things. Finances even, they think they're worse. And so we live in this completely delusional space. You know, as Steve Jobs used to talk about the distorted reality or the distorted reality field, well, all those entrepreneurs live there. Right. And it does piss me off when I see all these people because it is the fear of missing out and being like, man, you are like, you're missing out on your ability to achieve your goals this year. And you missed them three years in a row. What's going to change? Right. Right. Yeah. That's a massive point. And you know, what I'm seeing here and what I just want to also draw attention to is the parallels between what you're doing at scale versus what a coach does in a one-on-one situation. They will challenge the hell out of you. They will pull you out of your delusion and get you extremely real with what is present and actually happening. So you know, what I admire about Scott's marketing and what I really challenge everybody to do is take those elements and really do that at scale. When you are writing those emails, when you are writing those landing pages, when you are delivering those videos, you're speaking to that one person and you're assuming all that baggage and delusion they've brought to that situation and you're addressing that full on. My question to Scott, is this something that like you just came naturally into doing or is this something you've trained yourself to do in a more strategic way? Most of the shit I do is not strategic. That's relieving. Yeah. Yeah. I mean, you know, I'll be honest. I've been an entrepreneur since I was seven, 25 now. So it's like, you know, I've had, there's always something comes you can fail. Okay. There's always something, at a certain point, the chances of you failing are so small that you pretty much just end up winning at everything. Okay. And I definitely do not win at everything by any stretch of imagination, but I have a really good chance at this point. It's because I have so many data points of things that have gone wrong or experiences I've seen or whatever it may be. So what I say that most things that I do are strategic, looking back at them, they look like that. At the time, not really, I just kind of tinkered around, look at, you know, saw something that worked and, you know, double down on it. I'm really good at doubling down on things once I know they're working. But strategic, do I sit in a room kind of thinking for 12 hours about how I'm going to do something? You know, I don't think about most things more than two minutes. You know, it's like two minutes, yes or no, right? I do not spend much time on my decision making. And what I love about that is I get a lot of data points, you know what I mean? Like I really want to be able to die. Right? I really want to be able to die being like, man, you know, look at all the data points I got in my lifetime. Look at all the things I learned. Look at all the things that I screwed up. Look at all the things that didn't screw up. Look at all the things that I will never have to be like, what if? I'll be like, you know what? Yeah, I regret it making that decision. Man, oh man, this is good to know. So yeah, I mean, you know, I don't think that I don't think I make decisions perhaps as strategic as I could. But here's the other thing. As entrepreneurs, a lot of times we don't have the data points to make strategic decisions. We just have to make decisions and we have to stick by them and we have to be all in on that decision and not second guess ourselves. And I think that's what majority people, you know, think about it. If you're, you know, what's the number one reason why people have... I guess, sorry about that little tech glitch. That's what happens, you know, when Scott goes on these awesome little, not tangents, I think he just totally blew the Zoom line. So I think we're good right now. And yeah, we're going to handle this Zoom thing. Scott, go all out. Just do your thing. What was I even talking about? I don't even remember what I was talking about anymore. I definitely do go on tangents, but people enjoy them. So I keep on doing them until, you know, people say they don't want them anymore. Yeah. But do you remember? Because I don't remember. It blew my mind. It blew Zoom's mind. So I'm going to restart with another question that's just been really pressing on my mind actually. So one thing I've noticed, especially over the last few years, we teach a lot of it at Copyhackers is the whole Gene Schwartz model, the levels of awareness, really acknowledging your customer's existing context. One thing that I'm really challenging, you know, business owners, entrepreneurs to consider is that in 2017, it's not enough to know their level of awareness. You also have to ramp up and intensify their depth of awareness for a distracted world. You alluded to it with your omnipresence. You know, being there with the message, making sure that they remain focused on the challenge that must be solved instead of letting that attention dissipate. So I kind of want to pick your brain a little bit about some other strategies you've used to really dial up that intensity, make sure they remain laser focused on what needs to be shifted. Yeah. I mean, it really goes back to my methodology, the SSF method, S-S-F method, sidewalks, rolling fast lane, which really kind of alludes to the fact that there's, you know, there's a few different levels of awareness, but there's a sidewalk to slowing the fast lane of awareness. Sidewalk, somebody doesn't really even know that there's a pain. The pain is not relevant to them, let's call it. All in, somebody has the pain's relevant. They're looking for the process or the mechanism behind, you know, how do I make this pain, not be a pain? They're looking up to an authority at this point, which is a great place to be able to be, you know, in a place of, you know, knowing I can trust someone. And then in the fast lane, they're ready to actually, you know, engage in a solution, right? They actually want to, you know, kind of take the next step. And so what you've got to realize, every single piece of your marketing, whether it's a guide or a video series or a webinar or a sales page or an assessment or a quiz or whatever, it's all within one of these points. The sidewalks slowing fast lane. And that means that our messaging and how we talk to someone and how we engage with them and how long something is, the delivery stop, all these different types of things change based on which lane somebody is in. And so a lot of the time, most people just go for the fast lane, right? Majority of people just go for the fast lane. And of course, that's where we miss 98, 97% of all the people that could actually potentially become customers of ours. And so, and this comes back to the fact that majority of business owners, especially those making less than a million, are so short term focused, right? They're very short term focused. And it makes sense. You got 15, 30, 45 days of money in the bank and they're like, man, I need the next customer, the next customer, next customer. And what majority of people do is they only invest in short term solutions when in reality they need to be working the short term and the long term. Well, you know, if you're working the long term and the short term at the same time and you're marketing to the sidewalk to slow lane and the fast lane, you're sending emails to the sidewalk to slow lane and the fast lane, you're delivering Facebook ads to the sidewalk to slow lane and the fast lane, all simultaneously. This sounds a lot more complicated than it actually is. Well, what actually happens is now you're creating this momentum effect, this waterfall effect where the people that you're making aware of the pain, you know, becomes into the slow lane or the people that are in the slow lane come into the fast lane and the people that are in the fast lane, you know, become customers and now you're constantly expanding, expanding, expanding, expanding. And this is where you see businesses blow up overnight because of the fact that they're spending time, money and resources, being able to be omnipresent to the person in the sidewalks, slowing fast lane, all simultaneously. And it creates an amazing business situation and an amazing business because it's a long term focused business, not just let's just try to get the clients that are right or the people that are ready to, you know, buy today. Right, right. Exactly. Um, and I just want to add to this point, you know, one of my biggest, most exciting inquiries in marketing is, you know, how do we take, um, you know, the two to three percent that online marketing, you know, currently generates, you know, as a conversion conservatively and 10 X that to the 30 to 50 percent that one on one enrollment calls typically get, you know, what is that 10 X gap and how do we start bridging that at scale? And, you know, one of the core elements of that is simply, you know, on a basic level having the right conversation with the right person at the right time, in essence, being relevant. And that is why, you know, I absolutely love what Scott is doing with his SSF method, because I really do feel like it is one of those core things we can do to start bridging that gap. Um, is that kind of your experience of it is, do you feel like the SSF method is really, um, taking a lot of what works in that one on one scenario and bringing it down to something we could do at scale? I think, I think it's the best way. So, you know, let me, let me, let me say it in this way. Um, is there better ways to do it? Yes. Are they complicated and expensive? Yes. So the business making less than $20 million a year, I think it's the best methodology there is. Uh, you know, I have met, I have theories of what could work better that I've yet to be able to test out. Um, and, and, and so, you know, I, I, I don't talk about this. I don't talk about fairies. I talk about facts and results because that's what's real. But for the typical business, we always want to go in majority business are stuck in one, one, one, the few. We always want to go one to many, but there's a, there's of course, there's impact, there's intimacy and then the scale, right? There's all those three things that, and you have to, you have to figure out which one, which where, where, where they kind of fit for you. You know what I mean? Um, and you know, can you, can you close a $10,000 sale on a webinar? Maybe, maybe not. Uh, can you close a $2,000 sale on a webinar? Maybe, maybe not. Uh, so I, I, I, I, you know, I think, I think there's a lens of thinking that we can't replace intimacy, but at a certain level, we can scale at least some intimacy. And, and it's always going to come back to more sales because the more intimate you feel with somebody, um, the higher the chance that you're actually going to buy from them. Absolutely.
